title: REST APIs
purpose: Design predictable HTTP interfaces between clients and services.
outcomes: Explain REST APIs concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important REST APIs features
Implement, test and debug practical REST APIs solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of REST APIs before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: Resources, URLs and HTTP methods
topics.definition: A REST-style API models addressable resources over HTTP and uses standard methods, status codes, headers and representations to communicate state transitions.
topics.whyItMatters: Clients and services need a stable, understandable contract that can evolve independently.
whenToUse: Resource-oriented request-response APIs
Operations that fit HTTP caching and idempotency semantics
Public or cross-team contracts that benefit from broad tooling
whenNotToUse: Forcing every domain command into awkward CRUD
Using GET for state changes
Returning 200 for every success and failure
internalWorking: A client resolves DNS, creates a secure connection and sends an HTTP request
Routing selects a handler that authenticates, validates and calls domain logic
The service reads or changes data and serializes a representation
Intermediaries may cache or retry only when method semantics allow it
tradeoffs: Simple interoperable contracts versus over-fetching or multiple round trips
Stateless services scale easily but each request needs sufficient context
Versioning protects clients but increases maintenance
commonMistakes: Leaking database entities as permanent API contracts
topics.practicalExample: POST /assessments creates and returns 201 with a Location header; GET retrieves it; PATCH updates selected fields; DELETE returns 204 when removal succeeds.

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of Resources, URLs and HTTP methods.
interviewQuestions.answer: REST uses HTTP semantics around resources and representations. I design stable nouns, correct method and status behavior, consistent errors, validation and pagination. Idempotency matters for retries: PUT and DELETE should have the same intended effect when repeated, while POST usually needs an idempotency strategy.

interviewQuestions.answer: Forcing every domain command into awkward CRUD Using GET for state changes Returning 200 for every success and failure
followUps: What simpler alternative could replace Idempotency, and what would be lost?

interviewQuestions.question: How does Idempotency work internally?
interviewQuestions.answer: A client resolves DNS, creates a secure connection and sends an HTTP request Routing selects a handler that authenticates, validates and calls domain logic The service reads or changes data and serializes a representation Intermediaries may cache or retry only when method semantics allow it
followUps: How would you test and monitor Idempotency?

interviewQuestions.question: What are the main tradeoffs of Idempotency?
interviewQuestions.answer: Simple interoperable contracts versus over-fetching or multiple round trips Stateless services scale easily but each request needs sufficient context Versioning protects clients but increases maintenance
followUps: Which tradeoff matters most under production load?

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of Idempotency.
interviewQuestions.answer: REST uses HTTP semantics around resources and representations. I design stable nouns, correct method and status behavior, consistent errors, validation and pagination. Idempotency matters for retries: PUT and DELETE should have the same intended effect when repeated, while POST usually needs an idempotency strategy.
